chemicals.name: Based on the provided sources, pastries such as radish cake, rice cake, pineapple cake

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: The sources do not specify a toxic dose but warn that because these foods are "mostly made of glutinous rice and high starch," the entire food item is fundamentally unsuitable for a cat's digestive system
chemicals.description: in cats: Traditional festive pastries are primarily formulated from glutinous rice and high amounts of starch1. Because cats are obligate carnivores, their digestive systems are not equipped to break down and metabolize heavy loads of complex carbohydrates1. Ingesting these ingredients easily leads to severe flatulence (bloating) and acute gastrointestinal discomfort1. Any notes on concentration o
chemicals.name: High Starch and Glutinous Rice (Complex Carbohydrates)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: N/A. The danger is not dose-dependent but relies entirely on the physical consistency and stickiness of the pastry
chemicals.description: in cats: Because of how items like mochi and rice cakes are prepared, they possess a highly sticky and dense texture1. Rather than chemically poisoning the cat, this presents a severe physical choking hazard1. If a cat attempts to swallow these sticky foods, they can easily become lodged in the throat or esophagus, triggering a life-threatening emergency1. Any notes on concentration or potency: N/
chemicals.name: Highly Sticky Dough (Physical Hazard)

description: Pastries such as radish cake, rice cake, pineapple cake, and mochi are not acutely toxic to cats, but they are inappropriate foods that can cause digestive and metabolic problems. These products are high in refined carbohydrates and sugar — nutrients cats have no biological need for — and some varieties contain fillings or flavourings that may be harmful.
emergencyNote: Because the provided sources do not identify a specific chemical poison in pastries
name: Pastries (radish cake, rice cake, pineapple cake, mochi)
safetyNotes: Not toxic: plain versions without dangerous additives cause only digestive upset
Sugar and starch risk: cats cannot efficiently metabolise large carbohydrate loads; regular feeding leads to obesity and blood sugar dysregulation
Hidden hazards: check for xylitol (in sugar-free versions), onion or garlic powder, chocolate filling, or excessive salt
Mochi and similar sticky rice products: choking hazard and intestinal obstruction risk if eaten in large pieces
A small accidental taste is not an emergency; regular feeding of pastries is strongly discouraged
scientific_name: Pastries (radish cake, rice cake, pineapple cake, mochi)

symptoms.name: Based on the sources, pastries such as radish cake, rice cake, pineapple cake, and mochi do not contain a specific chemical poison
Flatulence and Gastrointestinal Discomfort
symptoms.notes: These pastries are primarily made from high amounts of starch and glutinous rice12. A cat's digestive system is not well-equipped to process heavy loads of complex carbohydrates, which makes these items difficult to digest and leads to acute stomach upset and gas
symptoms.onset: Within a few hours of ingestion (as digestion begins).

symptoms.name: Choking / Airway Obstruction
symptoms.notes: Because of their preparation, these foods—especially mochi and rice cakes—possess a highly sticky and dense physical texture12. This stickiness presents a severe physical hazard, as the pastry can easily become lodged in the cat's throat, triggering a life-threatening emergency
symptoms.onset: Immediate (during the act of chewing and swallowing).

treatments.name: Because the provided sources do not identify a specific chemical poison in pastries

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the sticky food mass becomes lodged in the cat's throat or creates a blockage in the digestive tract, the veterinarian must intervene immediately to clear the airway, or potentially perform surgery to safely remove the obstructing material12. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Emergency Airway Clearance / Obstruction Removal (If choking occurs)
treatments.notes: The sources explicitly warn that the dense, highly sticky texture of these festive pastries (especially mochi and rice cakes) presents a severe choking hazard for pets, making this a hyper-acute, life-threatening emergency

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the cat successfully swallows the pastry but develops severe gastrointestinal distress, the veterinarian will provide symptomatic and supportive care (such as fluid therapy or anti-nausea medications) to keep the cat stable and hydrated until the inflammation and symptoms resolve13. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Symptomatic Gastrointestinal Support (For severe bloating and GI upset)
